narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 29, lower-middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 70.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, was the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance ranked in the bottom tenth of your school. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, compared to an average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often correlate with bar exam challenges. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction a bit toward passing, showing some resilience in your college record. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often have decile rankings in the middle or upper ranges and stronger LSAT scores around 37, your early and mid-school performance stood out as unusually low, outweighing other elements like your full-time status, which is common among 93% of applicants. While your LSAT and family background played roles, they weren't the top deciders here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above 29, as it could shift future predictions in your favor, and reflect on strategies to improve your academic deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or community college courses that demonstrate upward trends. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these gaps, so use this feedback as a roadmap to strengthen your profile for the next cycle.
